reference: Flay BR, Miller TQ, Hedeker D, Siddiqui O, Britton CF, Brannon BR, et al. The television, school, and family smoking prevention and cessation project. VIII. Student outcomes and mediating variables. Prev Med. 1995;24(1):29-40.
activity_script_v1_1: Curriculum contains 10 sessions on smoking prevention delivered across two weeks. Several key elements to week one: (a) correction of norms (particularly misperceptions of the prevalence of tobacco use among adolescents and in the general population); (b) awareness of peer influences to smoke; (c) development of peer resistance skills; (f) instruction on the social and physiological consequences of smoking; and (g) development of decision-making skills. For week two: (a) motivations for smoking, (b) adult, advertising and media influences, (c) assertiveness and nonsmoker's rights.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: The importance of a reciprocal approach from schools staff was emphasised. Changes were made in response to pilot test results included decreased emphasis on exercise, diet, and relaxation tips, and more emphasis on self-talk, recycling, social support processes, and trigger situations.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2:
Regarding prevention, the TV delivery (F = 36.58, P < 0.001) demonstrated significant positive effects on overall program acceptance. Regarding cessation, the classroom effect (F = 7.35, P < 0.007) was somewhat stronger than the TV delivery effect (F = 5.29, P < 0.02).

Teachers shared a preference for social resistance curriculum that included classroom skills demonstration. Health instructors and students reported high acceptance overall.

reference: Flay BR, Miller TQ, Hedeker D, Siddiqui O, Britton CF, Brannon BR, et al. The television, school, and family smoking prevention and cessation project. VIII. Student outcomes and mediating variables. Prev Med. 1995;24(1):29-40.
activity_script_v1_1: Family involvement using homework assignments that required parent participation and smoking cessation print materials provided to parents. In addition,  a revised cessation sequence begins with an introduction to the interactive processes of self management and social support by assigning the Smoker's Diary exercise and the solicitation of a family member or friendship support commitment. The segment is tagged to the last day of the first prevention week. The program urges seventh grade viewers to volunteer to support parents who smoke and want to quit. Students whose parents are smoke-free are encouraged to support an older sibling, adult extended family member, or friend who is motivated to quit smoking.

reference: Flay BR, Miller TQ, Hedeker D, Siddiqui O, Britton CF, Brannon BR, et al. The television, school, and family smoking prevention and cessation project. VIII. Student outcomes and mediating variables. Prev Med. 1995;24(1):29-40.
activity_script_v1_1:
Television programming for social resistance skills training, smoking prevention, and smoking cessation. The program encouraged discussion (a) among students in the classroom, (b) between students and parents for both prevention and cessation in the home, and (c) among adults in the home and workplace for cessation. The programming was the KABC-TV "Feeling Fine" program, a health issues segment of the local evening news broadcast at 5:00 and 11:00 PM, Monday through Friday. Project research staff and staff from the KABCTV affiliate collaborated in the development of the videotaped segments. Each brought unique perspectives to the program development process. Research staff and television station staff shared a commitment to the goals of smoking prevention and cessation. The result was a three week program.

Prevention week I. The final sequence for the prevention TV series conformed to that of the classroom curriculum. Each segment capsulized and dramatized key points from the classroom curriculum. The final sequence began with presentation of inaccurate normative estimates. Social resistance to various degrees of direct pressure to use drugs was then presented. Resistance to direct pressure (Peer Influences) was followed by training in resistance to indirect pressure (Resistance to Effects of Passive Smoke). In the Adult Influences session, presented at the end of Week 1, students were taught to react assertively to direct and indirect pressure in the home to smoke cigarettes and to offer to support parent cessation efforts. Student cognitive and behavioral support of parents who were motivated to quit took place during the parent cessation week immediately following the first week of prevention. The sequence rationale for the televised cessation program combined self-management and social support with an emphasis on maintenance and the development of personalized recycling strategies in coping with relapse. The
social support concept was illustrated in the introduction of five examples of typical support relationships: (a) support provided by a seventh-grade project participant to his parents, both of whom smoked, (b). support provided by a seventh-grade project participant to his father who smoked, (c) spousal support with additional support provided by an older adolescent, (d) social support in the workplace provided by supervisor and colleagues, and (e) a single parent with support provided by teenage children. The quitting process was documented, with
each social unit illustrating a phase in the cessation process. 

Prevention Week 2. The second prevention week began with a review of assertive resistance to peer pressure and analysis of adult and adolescent motives for smoking. Resistance to direct pressure was then contrasted with pressure from entertainment media, the advertising industry, and celebrity opinion leaders. Advertising content analysis and critical viewing skills were taught as response techniques. Social activism was encouraged. The program concluded with decision making and personal commitments to not smoke and to not influence others to do so directly or indirectly. Thus, social resistance to direct peer and indirect adult pressure to smoke, presented in Week 1, prepared adolescents not only to resist personal pressure, but also to provide social support to parents and other adults during the following cessation week. While cessation participants used social support from children
and other family members in the home, the support network was also extended to the workplace. In a fashion analogous to the student development of social resistance to pressure to smoke in the first prevention week, during the following cessation week parents develop self-management techniques to cope with pressures inherent in high-risk situations following initial cessation. The program emphasized self-efficacy and recycling in the event of relapse and viewing cessation as an iterative process. 

After a 3-week break, the program culminated in the third week which recapitulated motives for smoking by contrasting adult and adolescent perspectives, promoted social activism in coping with media pressures, and
encouraged thoughtful decision-making leading to commitments to neither smoke nor influence others to do so.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
Unfortunately, the realities of a commercial news organization and labor contracts did not allow for the intended collaboration of research and production staff to deliver programming that demonstrated effective substance-use resistance techniques independent of the classroom sessions. 
Station and research staff also perceived the production process from different perspectives. Station staff expected that research staff would limit their involvement in videotape development and production to provision of prevention and cessation curriculum content and background information to station staff. Research staff thought it important to be involved throughout the production process to ensure program integrity. Both groups accommodated substantially to the other's needs and modus operandi. The physician host and program producer met on a weekly basis with research staff to discuss objectives, segment by segment. They were receptive to research staff recommendations regarding translation of objectives to scripts. However, research staff did not participate directly in script development, shooting, and editing. Local station policy and trade union restrictions
precluded involvement in final production activities by any but station management staff and union members. Also, unlike televised dramatic presentations, news broadcasts convey events as they occur. Hence, they must be videotaped or filmed in natural settings without coaching of participants. Thus, the demonstration of progressive acquisition of behavioral skills through roleplay was difficult to accomplish on videotape because research staff were not permitted to be present during shooting or editing. Additional practical problems occurred because daily
camera crews were assigned randomly, and the program director did not know who the crew would be until the day the shooting was to take place. Also, inclement weather caused cancellation of one key shoot that could not be rescheduled in time for broadcast, thus requiring modification of the cessation curriculum at the last minute.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2:
Regarding prevention, the TV delivery (F = 36.58, P < 0.001) demonstrated significant positive effects on overall program acceptance. Regarding cessation, the classroom effect (F = 7.35, P < 0.007) was somewhat stronger than the TV delivery effect (F = 5.29, P < 0.02).

The average rating across the 3-weeks of programming was 10.2. This translates to an average of half a million households, or about 650,000 people, seeing each segment.

Teams drafted their own ordinances (aimed at ensuring merchant compliance with tobacco age-of-sale laws and reducing youth access to tobacco) based on models from other communities, introduced the ordinance to their
city council, and then marshaled the support of community leaders and the public for their proposal. Team members and community supporters lobbied city councilors, met with tobacco retailers, and attempted to obtain the support of law enforcement officials for the proposed ordinance.

activity_script_v1_1: All communities increased the license fee to cover administrative costs, added a graduated system of civil penalties for the license holder, and banned tobacco vending machines, and 6 required that at least 2 unannounced compliance checks be carried out annually. In addition, 5 communities prohibited self-service displays of tobacco products, and 4 included fines for both salespersons who made illegal sales and minors who attempted to purchase tobacco.

reference: Gautam J, Glover M, Scragg R, Bullen C, Gentles D, Nosa V, et al. Parental and retail supply of tobacco to minors: findings from a community-based social supply intervention study. Health Policy. 2014;117(1):120-7.
activity_script_v1_1:
We hosted or attended local events to meet and engage with parents, and had a stall at locally run sports or smokefree days in the community, for example at a local marae (traditional Maori meeting place). 

We ran a family fun day.

A wallet card carrying the message "Don't sell or offer cigarettes to children" and "Report under 18 sales Ph: Smokefree Officer" on the front, and messages which which encouraged parents to keep cigarettes and butts out of children's reach on the back, was designed by the study team. The cards were distributed to parents and adults during school and health promotion events, and community meetings, to encourage people to report tobacco sale or supply to minors.

reference: Gautam J, Glover M, Scragg R, Bullen C, Gentles D, Nosa V, et al. Parental and retail supply of tobacco to minors: findings from a community-based social supply intervention study. Health Policy. 2014;117(1):120-7.
activity: 2.1 Establishing a coalition and building its capacity
activity_script_v1_1:
The Keeping Kids Smokefree (KKS) team sought to establish a group of representatives from the intervention
schools (board members, staff, and students), community (parents, local business association), health provider representatives and researchers, prior to intervention commencement to participate in the intervention design.
barriers_v1_1_v2: This activity was rejected by school representatives who expected the workload to be too great. They seemed to expect KKS to present with a predetermined set of expectations that to varying degrees would stretch the schools' capacity.

reference: Gautam J, Glover M, Scragg R, Bullen C, Gentles D, Nosa V, et al. Parental and retail supply of tobacco to minors: findings from a community-based social supply intervention study. Health Policy. 2014;117(1):120-7.
activity_script_v1_1:
Two "Quit and Win" type competitions were run, which have been used elsewhere with some success. KKS' "Sponsor To Win" competition encouraged students to find someone who smoked to quit for them by World Smokefree Day (31 May). For each sponsor who succeeded at quitting, the student was entered into a draw to win one set of prizes per intervention school with the first and major prize being a home computer package. Another competition was run in the last school term of the year solely for school board members and staff. Weekly sessions were also held at each school for parents and teachers to meet KKS intervention staff for support to quit.

A smokefree art competition was favored as it involved the whole student body. The winning art works produced
by students were used in the design of the KKS logo, website, and promotional material for the intervention, such as the wallet card for parents, posters for community displays, and advertising on buses.

Sponsored events at each school including subsidized food and drink, celebrity appearances, prize draws, spot prizes, and give-aways of smokefree merchandise, sporting and class-based fun activities for the students, cultural dance and music performances by the students, displays of students' art from the competition, on-the-spot smoking cessation assessments, and enrollment with Maori cessation support services.

The program and its key messages were promoted on banners, via a promotional character "Balmy Palmy" (e.g. a
palm tree costume inspired by our logo), acknowledgments over the public address system, and, supported by short talks by celebrities and study personnel.

reference: Gautam J, Glover M, Scragg R, Bullen C, Gentles D, Nosa V, et al. Parental and retail supply of tobacco to minors: findings from a community-based social supply intervention study. Health Policy. 2014;117(1):120-7.
activity_script_v1_1:
A DVD was produced, called Our Choice, Their Future that involved children from the intervention schools and celebrities. Each child was given a copy to give to their parents for viewing. a range of strategies was used to prompt
quit attempts among parents and school staff who smoked. At intervals throughout the year, material was sent to parents who were identified from the data as smokers to prompt and assist quit attempts.

Weekly sessions were also held at each school for parents and teachers to meet intervention staff for support to quit.

Quarterly newsletters, personal letters and notices were sent home with students, and a website (www.keepingkidssmokefree.org.nz) was set up to inform parents. Consistent with Maori and Pacific cultural preferences for face-to-face contact, a guiding principal was to create as many opportunities as possible to meet parents so they could see and assess who they were dealing with. Thus, written and electronic communication strategies were supplemented with direct communication; for example, presentations to school staff, parents, and community groups, and showings of the DVD at community libraries during school holidays.
risks_v1_1_v2: We found that at follow-up households in the intervention group were more likely than the control group to leave cigarettes and associated paraphernalia where minors could access them. The intervention components, and regular newsletters constantly reminded parents in the intervention group of the importance of keeping cigarettes out of children's reach, and may have raised their awareness of this behaviour making it more likely that they would recall at the time of the follow-up survey placement of tobacco products around the home.

reference: Gautam J, Glover M, Scragg R, Bullen C, Gentles D, Nosa V, et al. Parental and retail supply of tobacco to minors: findings from a community-based social supply intervention study. Health Policy. 2014;117(1):120-7.
activity_script_v1_1: The Auckland Regional Public Health Service conducts controlled purchase operations (CPOs) in response to complaints about tobacco product sales to minors. We provided a proactive program of CPOs in the intervention area. Within the intervention schools' catchment area, tobacco retailers in close proximity to the schools or on a major thoroughfare for students traveling to and from the schools were targeted. Two monthly CPOs have been shown to be more effective than 4 or 6 monthly visits. CPOs were therefore increased in frequency from once to twice a year in the second year of the intervention.
barriers_v1_1_v2: Enforcement checks using supervised minors often underestimate young people's ease of access to cigarettes. In addition, retailers have been found to be likely to sell to familiar minors, especially if they had been warned of possible enforcement checks. Retailers in the KKS study did receive such warning, and supervised minors were strangers to the area where CPOs were being conducted. As a result, the way CPOs was conducted may have failed to influence retailers' behaviour towards smoker minors who attempt to buy cigarettes.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Some authors have suggested that minors who tried to buy cigarettes were likely to behave differently from supervised minors used in enforcement checks. For example, minors are likely to lie about their age, use false ID, dress to appear older and cajole a clerk into making a sale. Supervised minors in the KKS study were instructed to dress in age appropriate clothes, tell the truth about their age and say that the cigarettes were for their personal use if asked and indicate that they were not carrying ID if asked.

reference: Gautam J, Glover M, Scragg R, Bullen C, Gentles D, Nosa V, et al. Parental and retail supply of tobacco to minors: findings from a community-based social supply intervention study. Health Policy. 2014;117(1):120-7.
activity_script_v1_1: Poster developed from a student's entry in the sponsored art competition carried the message "Don't sell or offer cigarettes to children," and "Report under 18 sales Ph: Smokefree Officer" including the phone number to call. The posters were distributed to all retailers along with an information pack containing an explanation of why retailers should not supply tobacco to children and information about the Smoke-free Environments Amendment Act.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Retailer compliance with sales-to-minors laws is dependent upon enforcement. Research suggests that active enforcement is required and monitoring needs to occur as regularly as bi-monthly to have an effect on reducing minor access to tobacco from retailers.
risks_v1_1_v2: Regarding retailer compliance to the tobacco sale law, students' report revealed that retailers in the intervention were less likely to ask for ID at the end of the study period. However, student reports may not be an accurate reflection of retailers' compliance behaviour as they are subject to response and recall bias. On the other hand, there was a reduction in minors' reported use of shops and purchase attempts. This behaviour change could be due to minors' exposure to the KKS strategy which may have reinforced to them that cigarette sales to minors is illegal, and that adults were informed to report those who sell. A similar observation was made in a previous study where although illegal sales decreased in both the intervention and control groups as a result of an active enforcement programme, the perception of ease of access and purchase attempt decreased only in the intervention group which were also exposed to community mobilization and awareness campaigns.

reference: Gautam J, Glover M, Scragg R, Bullen C, Gentles D, Nosa V, et al. Parental and retail supply of tobacco to minors: findings from a community-based social supply intervention study. Health Policy. 2014;117(1):120-7.

A smokefree art competition was favored as it involved the whole student body. The winning art works produced
by students were used in the design of the logo, website, and promotional material for the intervention, such as posters for community displays, and advertising on two buses.

The local leisure centre offered subsidised entry to smokers who pledged to give up smoking.

Youth centres arranged activities such as quizzes and video films and carbon monoxide testing on different evenings for different age groups.

activity_script_v1_1:
A hundred and seventy leaflets were taken home to families, and young people were made aware of the links between smoking and asthma and encouraged to create smoke-free zones in their homes. 32 no-smoking signs were taken home from one youth centre.

Each GP practice, city council area housing office and social services family centre and community centre in the intervention areas were asked to display the Brimingham Smokefree poster and postcards (Fast Facts) and leaflets (Thinking About Stopping).

reference: Gordon I, Whitear B, Guthrie D. Stopping them starting: evaluation of a community-based project to discourage teenage smoking in Cardiff. Health education journal. 1997;46:42-50.
activity: 2.1 Establishing a coalition and building its capacity
activity_script_v1_1: A comprehensive list of key workers from professions and organisations with potential to influence smoking behaviour of young people was drawn up, based on the literature and local knowledge. Individual contact was made with these workers in the intervention area to raise the profile of smoking-related issues in their work. No specific resources were available so the approach was to discuss specific interventions around smoking relevant to individual work practices.

reference: Gordon I, Whitear B, Guthrie D. Stopping them starting: evaluation of a community-based project to discourage teenage smoking in Cardiff. Health education journal. 1997;46:42-50.
activity_script_v1_1: The health promotion specialist and health education co-ordinators from two secondary schools developed a supplementary teaching resource consisting of a booklet and a 'take-home' worksheet for each pupil. The content was based on the requirements of the National Curriculum and the timing, in the first year of secondary school, to coincide with the new pressure from peers and older teenagers to soke.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
School nurses felt unable to provide smoking cessation counselling for older pupils and staff, undertake activities
involving parents for No Smoking Day or to lobby for smoke-free areas in the schools involved. They were hindered by a backlog of work created by a measles vaccination programme implemented in the previous term and they were trialling a new health review.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2: A questionnaire was sent to health education co-ordinators in the 23 schools who received the smoking education materials: 17 replied, and all, including those in the 8 study schools, reported that they had used them. The response indicated that the materials were appropriate for Year 7, and all schools wanted to receive the materials in future years.

reference: Gordon I, Whitear B, Guthrie D. Stopping them starting: evaluation of a community-based project to discourage teenage smoking in Cardiff. Health education journal. 1997;46:42-50.
activity_script_v1_1: Two sessions on substance misuse for parents were held at one of the schools and the tobacco issue was raised. Parents received leaflets on smoking and asthma and encouraged to have smoke free homes.

reference: Gordon I, Whitear B, Guthrie D. Stopping them starting: evaluation of a community-based project to discourage teenage smoking in Cardiff. Health education journal. 1997;46:42-50.
activity_script_v1_1: Two areas were selected for test purchases by the Trading Standards Department during the period of the intervention and breaches in the tobacco advertising voluntary code were noted.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
Visits to retailers uncovered under-age sales as an emotive issue with retailers. Many felt vulnerable and feared abuse and intimidating racial harassment if they refused to sell, leading to a desire to avoid arguments with teenagers.

Visiting retailers, maintaining awareness of local tobacco advertising and inter-agency links require a considerable time commitment over a long period.
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
No prosecutions were made. The trading standards officers concluded that tobacco retailers were aware of the under-age sales law and the penalties that could be served on defaulters.

Visiting the retailers to maintain awareness of local tobacco advertising and inter-agency links showed some benefit in restricting under-age sales rather than sole reliance on test purchases alone.

reference: Gordon I, Whitear B, Guthrie D. Stopping them starting: evaluation of a community-based project to discourage teenage smoking in Cardiff. Health education journal. 1997;46:42-50.
activity_script_v1_1: Contact was made with community police officers who informed neighbourhood-watch groups in two of the intervention areas about the under-age sales law. All tobacco retailers in the four areas (n=89) were visited, reminded of the under-age sales law, sent the statutory notice if they did not have one displayed and offered leaflets to hand to young people to explain refusal to sell cigarettes.

reference: Gordon I, Whitear B, Guthrie D. Stopping them starting: evaluation of a community-based project to discourage teenage smoking in Cardiff. Health education journal. 1997;46:42-50.

activity_script_v1_1: One practice team organised a wide range of activities as a result of the intervention.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
The project also demonstrated the challenge to local specialist health promotion services in building a community-wide approach to health issues in local environments which place low priority on shifting existing resources into
primary prevention.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Such challenges demonstrate the value of health promotion specialists in being able to negotiate small local changes in practice to enable this shift.

activity: 2.1 Establishing a coalition and building its capacity
activity_script_v1_1: As a starting point, a community facilitator (NSW Cancer Council Health Education Officers with experience in community-based cancer reduction strategies) was recruited for each intervention town. The main role of the facilitator is to act as a link between the community and the research team. Facilitators were involved in organising the first community meeting and are responsible for ongoing liaison with the community committee and access-point networks. A balance of representatives across key community access points was sought (health care providers, community organisations, media, retailers, schools, and workplaces).

reference: Hancock L, Sanson-Fisher R, Perkins J, McClintock A, Howley P, Gibberd R. Effect of a community action program on adult quit smoking rates in rural australian towns: the CART project. Prev Med. 2001;32(2):118-27.

activity_script_v1_1:
A committee manual was developed as a guide to implementing community activities. Committees were
asked to convene at least every 2 months. Facilitators are expected to be present at all committee meetings to provide guidance and support. The committee was asked at the first meeting to nominate representatives to take responsibility for activation through the six key access-points: health care providers, community organisations, media, retailers, schools, and workplaces.
During the last 6 months of the program period, an attempt was made to accelerate activities by appointing a community liaison officer from within each town, to act as a resource for achieving program targets. Community liaison officers were supervised by facilitators in their day-to-day tasks, and were expected to be much more "hands-on" than facilitators.

activity_script_v1_1: In Year 1 and Year 2, the intervention included six peer-led, fun activities (e.g. films, street plays, games and role plays). These activities (a) taught youth about the health consequences of tobacco use; (b) helped them identify and resist social influences for tobacco use; and (c) empowered youth to advocate for tobacco-free homes and communities. Repeated sessions for each activity were carried out at strategic locations in the communities to reach the maximum number of youth.

activity_script_v1_1: Adult and youth leaders employed a community mapping technique to identify outdoor public spaces (e.g. parks, outdoor temples) where tobacco was commonly consumed. There, they posted a board that read 'Tobacco Free Zone' in the local Indian language, Hindi. The support group monitored these spaces and local tobacco vendors each fortnight, (i.e. every 2 weeks), for varied amounts of time.

activity_script_v1_1: Leaders engaged each tobacco vendor in the community to distribute minimum age warning boards for them to display. By law, these boards must contain the warning, 'Sale of tobacco products to a person under the age of 18 is a punishable offense' in the local Indian language.

reference: Harrell MB, Arora M, Bassi S, Gupta VK, Perry CL, Reddy KS. Reducing tobacco use among low socio-economic status youth in Delhi, India: outcomes from project ACTIVITY, a cluster randomized trial. Health Educ Res. 2016;31(5):624-38.
activity_script_v1_1: An SMS (i.e. text messaging) campaign was also implemented. Three SMSs per week for 4 months were sent to those registered for the campaign (~5% of each cluster). The campaign (a) raised awareness about the health effects of tobacco use and (b) promoted quitting behaviors.

reference: Harrell MB, Arora M, Bassi S, Gupta VK, Perry CL, Reddy KS. Reducing tobacco use among low socio-economic status youth in Delhi, India: outcomes from project ACTIVITY, a cluster randomized trial. Health Educ Res. 2016;31(5):624-38.
activity_script_v1_1: Day-long cessation camps were organized to provide access to individual and group counselling services for tobacco cessation. Project staff were trained in behavioral counseling techniques to provide support and guidance to tobacco users to help them quit smoking and/or using smokeless tobacco. These techniques were informed by prior evidence-based strategies employed in the West and India. The focus of these camps was to support young people in quitting tobacco use; however, other adults were also included to change community norms. Strategies included films, pictorial handouts, board games, cessation booklets and face-to-face interactions.

reference: Jøsendal O, Aarø LE, Torsheim T, Rasbash J. Evaluation of the school-based smoking-prevention program "BE smokeFREE". Scandinavian Journal of Psychology. 2005;46(2):189-99.
activity_script_v1_1:
In the classroom program, students were involved in eight sessions throughout the school-year. Main themes were personal freedom, freedom to choose, freedom from addiction, to make own decisions, training social skills to resist smoking pressure and short-term consequences of smoking. All activities were administered by the schools' own teachers in the normal classroom context... In Grades 7 to 9, the numbers of school hours allocated for the
intervention were eight, five, and six, respectively. Every school hour contained elements of non-traditional classroom activities such as the use of video, games, and group work. Activities were designed to ensure that all students would participate actively.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
During Grade 8, teachers and students indicated to the program administrators that the main messages and educational approaches that had been chosen when planning the intervention had been sufficiently emphasized, and requested a change. 

Students were allowed to make individual choices on activities on a number of occasions. Students performing below average at school probably found that the methods used were insufficiently structured and perhaps also too difficult and demanding.

The activity was carried out among students in one grade cohort only. We may hypothesize that the activity would have been even more effective if all grades had received the intervention.

reference: Jøsendal O, Aarø LE, Torsheim T, Rasbash J. Evaluation of the school-based smoking-prevention program "BE smokeFREE". Scandinavian Journal of Psychology. 2005;46(2):189-99.
activity_script_v1_1: Grade 9 students developed, carried out, and evaluated their own campaign to promote a smoke free lifestyle among Grade 7 students at their own school. Students were recruited to different working groups, which each held a particular role within the campaign at their own school. Students were given opportunities to express the advantages of being smoke-free through a variety of activities such as singing, writing, and making T-shirts.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
Students were allowed to make individual choices on activities on a number of occasions. Students performing below average at school probably found that the methods used were insufficiently structured and perhaps also too difficult and demanding.

The activity was carried out among students in one grade cohort only. We may hypothesize that the activity would have been even more effective if all grades had received the intervention.

reference: Jøsendal O, Aarø LE, Torsheim T, Rasbash J. Evaluation of the school-based smoking-prevention program "BE smokeFREE". Scandinavian Journal of Psychology. 2005;46(2):189-99.
activity_script_v1_1: Teacher training consisted of a 2-day course, where main elements of the program, its goals and methods were presented. Teachers were given detailed manuals in order to secure program fidelity, and teachers filled in a questionnaire immediately after each lesson in order to evaluate the lesson and evaluate program fidelity.
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
When teachers are attending courses, one might expect that they develop a deeper understanding of the program.
They are exposed to relevant experiences from other programs, they participate in discussions on the theoretical
background of the program, and they may develop a clearer understanding of the rationale underlying various program components. Furthermore, one might expect that the teachers attending courses are feeling more comfortable and skilled when administering the program in class. Participating in preparatory training may also lead to more enthusiasm and a stronger motivation for administering the program according to the instructions provided.

Norwegian teachers are at least to some extent familiar with educational approaches that include active involvement of students. There is a system of regular communication and meetings between teachers and parents.

reference: Jøsendal O, Aarø LE, Torsheim T, Rasbash J. Evaluation of the school-based smoking-prevention program "BE smokeFREE". Scandinavian Journal of Psychology. 2005;46(2):189-99.
activity_script_v1_1: Parents were involved in three different ways: 1) Two brochures were brought home by the students, the first one giving information about the project, the second giving advice about how to communicate with teenagers, and about how to say no to tobacco. 2) Teachers involved parents in discussions at appropriate occasions. 3) No-smoking contracts were signed by students and parents.
barriers_v1_1_v2: The activity was carried out among students in one grade cohort only. We may hypothesize that the activity would have been even more effective if all grades had received the intervention.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: The direct contact between teachers and parents, including parents who smoke may have contributed to mobilizing parental support for the campaign messages and intentions even among parents who smoke. Similar support is evidently more difficult to obtain through mass media campaigns.

activity_script_v1_1: The low and intensive media campaign levels included paid advertisements aimed at youth prevention, and contained age-appropriate messages disseminated through television, radio, print media and billboards as well as free public service announcements. The media campaign levels differed in the number and frequency of ad placement.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Program conditions combined with the intensive or low media campaign produced a reduction in beliefs about the beneficial effects of smoking, while a lack of media campaign resulted in an increase in beliefs about such benefits. The only unexpected exception was mood control benefits in the intensive media campaign/enhanced school program condition. This may be explained by the existence of outliers and significant variations in trends unrelated to the research design.

In Kansas City, the local name for the MPP is Project STAR (Students Taught
Awareness and Resistance).

From fall 1984 through fall 1985: A 10-session school program for resisting and counteracting drug use influences, and prevention practice homework activities with parents.
From Fall 1985 through Fall 1986: a 5-session booster school program for maintaining resistance skills practice,
counteracting influences anticipated in the transition to high school. Prevention practice homework activities with parents.

The school and booster programs were delivered in regular health, science, or
social studies classes by teachers, using modeling,
role-playing, group discussion,
and peer facilitation methods
of instruction.
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
The findings are robust, regardless of covariates, units, and statistical models used in analysis.
The findings do suggest, however, that program effects are somewhat diluted by
2-year follow-up, using the endpoint school as the unit of analysis.

The school environment effect does not appear to
be a function of grade, mixed experimental groups, or type of analysis model
used. However, an alternative explanation is that weaker program effects were
due to the event of the school transition rather than the new smoking environment
posed by the endpoint school.

activity_script_v1_1: The activity consisted of 7 peer-led classroom activities for 6th- and 8th-grade students in 2004-2005 and 6 additional activities for 7th- and 9th-grade students in 2005-2006. The objectives of the activity were to influence environmental factors (social norms, role models, social support, opportunities) and intrapersonal factors (knowledge, values, meanings, beliefs, skills) that predict tobacco use among urban Indian youths.
barriers_v1_1_v2: Although lessons on the use of chewing tobacco were emphasized, much more information was provided about smoking-related consequences, influences, norms, and policies. Given the high rates of chewing tobacco use in the sample and the high rates of morbidity and mortality among adults who chew tobacco, it is clear that additional, specific interventions are needed to address this form of tobacco use among young people.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2: Teachers were asked to rank the appropriateness of the program materials, classroom activities, parent postcards, and school posters on a scale of 1 (not appropriate) to 7 (very appropriate). The teachers overall ranked the appropriateness of the intervention components highly, with teachers from government schools (n = 67) ranking the components more highly than teachers from private schools (n = 49); the mean scores were 6.9 and 6.4, respectively. These data suggest that the translation process resulted in program materials that were rated as highly appropriate for Indian youth and families.

reference: Perry CL, Stigler MH, Arora M, Reddy KS. Preventing Tobacco Use Among Young People in India: Project MYTRI. Am J Public Health. 2009;99(5):899-906.
activity_script_v1_1: The peer leadership component focused on peer-led health activism outside of the classroom, including competitions between classrooms and schools that involved model building (crafting a three-dimensional model a tobacco-free school) and street play (an extended, culturally appropriate roleplay to practice refusal skills) competition for the 6th and 8th graders, respectively. Classroom activities were conducted in small groups of 10 to 15 and were led by students or peer leaders (students admired by their classmates, elected as so). These activities were designed to be interactive and enjoyable (e.g., games).

barriers_v1_1_v2: Activities have unfolded at such a rapid pace that establishing the necessary linkages between state agencies charged with implementing portions of AB 75 and community resources that serve target populations has proven more difficult than expected. Coordination and administration of interventions is labor intensive due to the sheer scale of the program. activity_script_v1_1: In response to a successful citizen-led ballot initiative, Proposition 99, the state of California increased taxes levied on cigarettes from 10 cents (one of the lowest in the country) to 35 cents per pack. For noncigarette tobacco products, a new tax of 42 cents on the dollar wholesale was levied, with the rate to be set annually by the State Board of Equalization.

reference: Pierce JP, Gilpin EA, Emery SL, White MM, Rosbrook B, Berry CC. Has the California Tobacco Control Program Reduced Smoking? JAMA : the journal of the American Medical Association. 1998;280(10):893-9.
activity_script_v1_1: Assembly Bill (AB) 75 was signed into law. This legislation specified the overall goal of the legislation to be a 75% reduction in tobacco consumption by the year 1999 and granted authority to the CDHS and the Department of Education to implement the medical care and health education portions of the proposition.
barriers_v1_1_v2: It is possible that lower funding for the Tobacco Control Program or increased expenditures by the tobacco industry for advertising and promotion played a role in the loss of the early program success. Beginning with fiscal year 1993-1994, there was a marked reduction in program funding. The annual average was $53.0 million, or $2.08 per capita, which was a reduction of 40% from the early years of the program. This reduction in the level of effort aimed at reducing smoking in California is a possible explanation for the loss of program effect.

Grant awards are authorized to school districts for nonclassroom activities and to other public and private nonprofit groups for development of innovative approaches that can be replicated to diminish tobacco use.

reference: Pierce JP, Gilpin EA, Emery SL, White MM, Rosbrook B, Berry CC. Has the California Tobacco Control Program Reduced Smoking? JAMA : the journal of the American Medical Association. 1998;280(10):893-9.
activity_script_v1_1:
A statewide televised cessation intervention, which will reach 3.1 million smokers and projects achievement of a 10% quit rate, is being funded.

The state's $28.6 million media campaign was launched in April 1990, with a central theme of reversing the erroneous image that smoking is glamorous that was created through decades of tobacco industry advertising. Campaigns that focused on minorities and youth began in May 1990 and continued throughout the summer months. Television, radio, print, and outdoor advertising are being used to target, in successive priority, the following population groups: children aged 11 to 14 years; children aged 6 to10 years; adolescents and young adults aged 15 to 21 years; adult smokers; pregnant women who smoke; and individuals, primarily men, who experiment with or regularly use smokeless tobacco.

Over the 18-month period, a total of $6.3 million worth of media will be produced, resulting in approximately 20 television spots that target the general market and specific ethnic groups (Black, Hispanic, Vietnamese, Korean, Chinese [produced in both the Cantonese and Mandarin dialects], and Japanese audiences). Fifty radio spots will be developed to target youth audiences. In addition to electronic media, outdoor advertising in shopping malls and on billboards and buses and exposures through the print media for younger children and specific minority groups are planned.
barriers_v1_1_v2: The tobacco industry engaged in a variety of political activities, which may have influenced the level of commitment of the state administration and legislature to the California Tobacco Control Program. Funding for the media campaign was vetoed by the governor in 1992 and later restored, but it was reinstated at a lower level than previously. Also, the administration has been accused of "watering down" the antismoking advertising.

activity_script_v1_1:
The legislation (AB 75) called for the CDHS' existing Child Health Disability Prevention Program, which provides health assessments primarily for children younger than 6 years who are eligible for Medicaid, to supplement existing activities with health education against tobacco use. With its appropriation, the Child Health Disability Prevention Program has been able to expand its program eligibility to children younger than 19 years whose family income is less than 200% of the federal poverty level. It has also adopted a protocol for use during health assessments that identifies tobacco users and those exposed to secondhand smoke, provides physician-delivered prevention and cessation messages, makes referrals for additional community prevention and cessation programs, and collects tobacco use and exposure data. In addition, the program provides assistance to the Child Health Disability Prevention Program providers increating smoke-free clinic environments.

In addition to nearly 17 projects that target pregnant women and programs that are being implemented at local sites in the state's Women, Infants, and Children Supplemental Food Program, some 50 family planning clinics are initiating tobacco use prevention programs.

reference: Mohlman MK, Boulos DN, El Setouhy M, Radwan G, Makambi K, Jillson I, et al. A randomized, controlled community-wide intervention to reduce environmental tobacco smoke exposure. Nicotine Tob Res. 2013;15(8):1372-81.
activity_script_v1_1:
Mosques and churches were engaged and trained in educating their communities about the hazards of smoking and environmental smoke (ETS, or second hand smoking) and in raising the issue of smoking as a sinful behavior (a position supported by current Islamic jurisprudence and the Coptic Orthodox Pope's advice).

Female social change agents (raedat refeyat) provided information to adult women in the home on the negative health effects of tobacco use and ETS. They also taught these women how to better protect themselves and their children from ETS through a standardized message sensitive to cultural family dynamics. This was done to avoid conflict with the smoker in the family, who was most often the husband. The intervention team relied solely on personal contacts with the women since some house holds did not have access to telephones.
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
The vast majority cited the home visits as the most effective activity. This finding is especially important in other culturally similar regions in the Middle East and North Africa, where social change agents have already been effective in promoting behaviors related to reproductive and child health and avian influenza prevention and where many women do not work outside the home. Reaching these women can be difficult through other channels, yet they can have major impacts on changing smoking behaviors and improving attitudes in the home toward positive health changes. Furthermore, the success of social change agents in environmental tobacco smoking (ETS) prevention could be replicated in other populations such as minority communities in the United States or migrant communities in European Union countries where language, traditions and customs, socioeconomics, and access
to education are barriers to accessing health care information and services.

reference: Mohlman MK, Boulos DN, El Setouhy M, Radwan G, Makambi K, Jillson I, et al. A randomized, controlled community-wide intervention to reduce environmental tobacco smoke exposure. Nicotine Tob Res. 2013;15(8):1372-81.
activity_script_v1_1: The study team met with community leaders to build support and ensure that the intervention could be executed smoothly. All those involved in implementing the intervention participated in training activities. All trainees received a guide booklet, a pamphlet on smoking and passive smoking hazards, and CDs with lectures on smoking hazards.

The Department of Community Health at the County Council further developed the program. They hosted the management and worked in close cooperation with four municipalities in the county. Regular checkups by regional and local County Council representatives were carried out to assure that the intervention components were delivered.

Some factors were prioritized when building the intervention: cooperation over sector borders; integration of the intervention into daily work; and local ownership and participation. Schools were encouraged to work closely with the local community, such as youth clubs, organizations, and shop owners.

The County Council worked as a booster-giving knowledge, inspiration and motivation to contribute to an intervention with continuous effectiveness and staying power.

It was felt that local ownership was essential in maintaining engagement for a long term intervention. Local ownership resulted in an intervention with a similar core, with some differences in local adaptations.

Classroom curriculum including increasing knowledge and awareness on tobacco-related issues, positive reinforcement by different rewards, and social support and influence from friends, parents, and significant
others. This included a 120 minute classroom lessons per year for four years, and one 90 minute lesson involving a County council representative for 90 minutes.

reference: Nilsson M, Stenlund H, Bergström E, Weinehall L, Janlert U. It takes two: reducing adolescent smoking uptake through sustainable adolescent-adult partnership. J Adolesc Health. 2006;39(6):880-6.
activity_script_v1_1: Before students completed the school year, they were given the opportunity to team up with a tobacco-free adult to form a tobacco-free pair-or "duo." The name Tobacco Free Duo originated from the idea that the pairs signed a contract to stay tobacco-free together for the next 3 years. The adult involved was thereby making a commitment to both provide a good example and to actively support the student to stay tobacco free. Informational meetings were held for the involved adults to provide knowledge and encouragement. The contracts were signed at a meeting at the end of grade six that all the pairs were invited to attend. The participating students were given a membership card valid until the end of grade nine, and local sponsors provided rewards of discounts and small prizes. The participating students and adults signed an assurance of fulfillment of the contract annually in grades seven through nine.
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
Effective programs need to be embedded in a developmental approach that attempts to reduce both early smoking experimentation and the effects of peer pressure on the development of cigarette smoking. In Tobacco Free Duo, attention was given to the school setting as a social system that could function as a supportive environment when interacting with other parts of the community. The surrounding society was addressed and supporting systems were created. A system-oriented and empowering approach, sensitive to internal as well as external influencing factors, was considered important in the program. The individual was addressed by the intervention, given a chance to develop and assert a personal decision, but put in a context where involvement and a feeling of ownership were essential. A dialogue with the target group assured that the intervention was based on the adolescents' reality.

In Tobacco Free Duo it was considered essential to show that the majority of the young decided to stay tobacco free. Doing it all together in tobacco-free pairs gave an imaginary audience, which could increase the possibility
that the adolescent identified with a healthy peer group. By working on a long-term, broad strategy involving
many people, the objective was to decrease the social acceptance for tobacco in the local society and establish a
tobacco-free norm. The intention was that the intervention would become a part of daily life.

It was felt that local ownership was essential in maintaining engagement for a long term intervention. Local ownership resulted in an intervention with a similar core, with some differences in local adaptations.

reference: Nilsson M, Stenlund H, Bergström E, Weinehall L, Janlert U. It takes two: reducing adolescent smoking uptake through sustainable adolescent-adult partnership. J Adolesc Health. 2006;39(6):880-6.

facilitators_v1_1_v2:
In teaching refusal skills, there might be a risk that the peer group could be considered a negative influence. Identification with a peer group can provide a positive psychosocial effect that, in turn, can prevent the use of tobacco. 

It was felt that local ownership was essential in maintaining engagement for a long term intervention. Local ownership resulted in an intervention with a similar core, with some differences in local adaptations.

activity_script_v1_1: Parents were informed about the harms of tobacco, and information was given about the importance of their taking a clear stance against the use of tobacco by their children and their children's friends. Parents were invited to sign a contract with their child to stay tobacco-free together for the next 3 years. They were thereby making a commitment to both provide a good example and to actively support their child to stay tobacco free. Informational meetings were held for the involved adults to provide knowledge and encouragement. In addition, a newsletter for the program was delivered twice a year.
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
Parents function as role models not only by being smokefree. The way they react to the children's smoking is also
very important to adolescent smoking uptake. A majority of adolescents express that parents should try and
influence children not to smoke. As part of this intervention, parents were invited to informational meetings and discussions on how to support their children in staying tobacco-free. A majority of the students chose their parents
as partners in a duo. Part of Tobacco Free Duo was addressing adolescent experimentation with tobacco by giving adult support in school.

It was felt that local ownership was essential in maintaining engagement for a long term intervention. Local ownership resulted in an intervention with a similar core, with some differences in local adaptations.

activity_script_v1_1: Using focus groups, we produced Vietnamese language health education materials, including three posters, a calendar, bumper stickers, lapel badges, and two brochures. One brochure targeted male smokers and described the health hazards of smoking and the benefits of quitting. The second brochure targeted females and described the health effects of environmental tobacco smoke on children and family members. We also produced a 32-page, four-colour, self-help "quit kit" that targeted those who wanted advice on how to stop smoking. Project staff re-supplied community businesses and agencies with brochures and kits.

reference: McPhee SJ, Jenkins CNH, Wong C, Fordham D, Lai KQ, Bird JA, et al. Smoking cessation intervention among Vietnamese Americans: a controlled trial. Tobacco control. 1995;4(Suppl 1):S16-S24.

activity_script_v1_1:
Newspaper articles about the health hazards of smoking, each of which was published in up to 10 different Vietnamese language newspapers and magazines. 

For the electronic media, we produced a 22 minute videotape in Vietnamese, which featured interviews with Vietnamese male smokers about their reasons for smoking, interviews with Vietnamese community physicians about the health hazards of smoking, and finally, interviews with Vietnamese male quitters about how they quit and how they refused cigarettes when offered. Six paid anti-tobacco television advertisements, based on segments of the videotape.

We launched an outdoor, print and electronic media counter-advertising campaign. For outdoor media, we developed Vietnamese language billboards of three different types which were posted in neighbourhoods with high concentrations of Vietnamese. One billboard targeted cigarette offering behaviour, the second focused on refusal skills, and the third billboard modelled quitting behaviour in a family context. The billboard targeting the offering of cigarettes was the first billboard ever to appear in the Vietnamese language in the US and drew considerable print and electronic media attention when it was first posted.
barriers_v1_1_v2: Media frequency and duration of exposure may not have been sufficient.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Media had a broad reach in the community.

activity_script_v1_1:
During the persuasive-behavioral sessions, students were taught about social pressures that may lead to smoking (peer, adults, and mass media) and were trained to deal with such pressures through role playing and other active learning techniques. Older peer leaders assisted in implementing the program at school. The short-term and long-term health hazards were also discussed during the program.
 
The sessions included three main topics. The first was making children aware of the reasons for smoking behavior. Children were informed about the impact of tobacco advertising, false image of smoking, social pressures among adolescents to start smoking, and adulthood, problems the of puberty, effects of parental and the development learning of tobacco addiction. The second topic was the health hazards of smoking. Peer leaders briefly explained the harmful effects of smoking. These issues, and the importance of not starting to smoke, were discussed by the children. The third topic was teaching the skills necessary to resist various pressures to begin smoking. The children were asked to write and to act out situations that might lead to smoking; they then tried to find solutions other than starting to smoke.
 
The program used older peer leaders as primary agents to deliver antismoking messages in a classroom setting. 5 behavioral classroom sessions were to be carried out by health education teachers at the schools who were trained as part of an effort to disseminate new curricula throughout North Karelia ("county-wide" program). In the county-wide intervention, a five-session series was held in about half of the schools.

The program used a social influence approach. Students were taught about social pressures to smoke, exerted by peers, adults, and mass media, and were trained by demonstration and role playing to deal with them. The short-term and long-term health hazards of smoking were also discussed during the program.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
It is somewhat surprising that the health educator-led program did not have stronger effects than the teacher-led
program after the immediate posttest. This finding may be a result of the communitywide program to which both sets of program schools were exposed.

reference: Puska P, Jaini P. The North Karelia Project: Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease in Finland Through Population-Based Lifestyle Interventions. Am J Lifestyle Med. 2020;14(5):495-9.

Local newspapers published articles on smoking. Many more were published about the prevention of heart disease in general. The local radio station was used to broadcast the project's specific non-smoking messages. During the same period anti-smoking leaflets were distributed in addition to other anti-smoking materials, including copies of signs and stickers with the message "Do not smoke here-we are in the North Karelia Project".

Local newspapers published articles on smoking and the prevention of heart disease in general. The local radio station was used to broadcast the project's specific non-smoking messages. Comprehensive smoking cessation programmes were broadcast on national television in 1978, 1979, 1980 and 1982. 

Based on the smoking cessation group course developed by the project, a television programme 'Keys to Health' was introduced and die first national television programme was launched in 1978. Ordinary smokers tried to stop smoking in a studio group on television. Usually one course contained 7-15 sessions on national television, each of diem lasting 30-45 min.

Each health department applicant was required to establish a tobacco control program, hire staff, and form broad-based coalitions with representation from area voluntary health agencies, professional associations and
medical societies, school districts and boards of education, community groups, employers, the media, and competitive grant awardees. After their start-up phase is completed, local health departments will further develop plans to deliver comprehensive prevention, cessation, and outreach services to low income, at-risk (by virtue of elevated prevalence of smoking rates or lack of access to care) populations.

barriers_v1_1_v2:
Between 1989 and 1996, the legislature underfunded the Proposition 99 Health Education programs by over $273 million. This underfunding occurred because the public health groups failed to exercise power, ideas, and the leadership needed for legislative success. Even successful litigation against the governor failed to restore the
programs. In July 1996, however, the under expenditures stopped because the issue of the diversions received significant media and public attention.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: The tobacco control groups used a variety of outsider strategies, including paid advertising, free media, and a grassroots campaign, and the leadership of these groups, in addition to the lobbyists, got involved in the campaign to secure implementing legislation. Without ongoing public pressure, it is likely that policy changes created by tobacco tax initiatives will dissipate into something acceptable to powerful insider interests, such as the tobacco and medical service provider industries.

reference: Pierce JP, Gilpin EA, Emery SL, White MM, Rosbrook B, Berry CC. Has the California Tobacco Control Program Reduced Smoking? JAMA : the journal of the American Medical Association. 1998;280(10):893-9.

activity_script_v1_1: Additional tax ($0.25 per pack) imposed across California
barriers_v1_1_v2: A set of internal memoranda from the Tobacco Institute surfaced. These internal memoranda, written in 1990, outlined a strategic plan for combatting the California Tobacco Control Program. The plan called for lobbying the California legislature to intervene, encouraging and supporting minority organizations to oppose the program, convincing the health services director to pull or modify media messages that reflected poorly on the industry, and encouraging the governor to intercede against the program. There is evidence that these strategies were used and met with some success. As mentioned previously, the governor initially vetoed the media budget in 1992, although he reconsidered following significant public pressure. Antismoking media funding was reduced by 50% for 1995-1996, and anti-industry media spots were short-lived. Furthermore, tobacco industry campaign contributions to legislature candidates, other elected officials, political parties, and political party committees totaled over $1.5 million in 1995-1996; this was a 70% increase compared with the level of such contributions in the 1993-1994 election cycle. On a per legislator basis, members of the California legislature received twice as much money as did members of the US Congress, even though California is not a tobacco-producing state. The slowing of the decline in smoking in recent years may well be a result of these political counterstrategies by the tobacco industry.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Active lobbying by health advocacy organizations and lawsuits against the administration brought by the American Lung Association, Americans for Nonsmokers' Rights, the American Cancer Society, and the American Heart Association brought significant public pressure on the Governor to reconsider vetoing the media budget.

reference: Pierce JP, Gilpin EA, Emery SL, White MM, Rosbrook B, Berry CC. Has the California Tobacco Control Program Reduced Smoking? JAMA : the journal of the American Medical Association. 1998;280(10):893-9.
activity_script_v1_1: Support for the adoption of ordinances at the local level that restricted or banned smoking in indoor workplaces and public places. California Assembly Bill 13 was enacted in January 1994, and it prohibited smoking statewide in 1995 in all indoor workplaces except bars, taverns, and casinos.
barriers_v1_1_v2: The tobacco industry was a major political player in the state legislature. Playing on several key themes-antiregulation, antitax, and freedom of choice-the tobacco industry had been able to protect itself for years (Glantz and Balbach 2000). There had been no tax increase on tobacco for over twenty years, reflecting the power of the tobacco industry and the comparative weakness of the voluntary health groups. The tobacco industry had spent $21.4 million in its unsuccessful bid to defeat Proposition 99 (Traynor and Glantz 1996) and would work as hard to influence its implementing legislation. The tobacco industry spent $2.2 million on lobbying the legislature in the 1987-1988 election cycle, which included the fight over Proposition 99, and $3.1 million in 1989-1990, when the legislature was considering implementing legislation for Proposition 99. By contrast, in the 1985-1986 election cycle, the tobacco industry had only spent $336,662 on lobbying. Between 1987-1988 and 1988-1989, tobacco industry campaign contributions to California legislators nearly doubled to $2.8 million (Begay and Glantz 1994; Begay, Traynor, and Glantz 1993; Glantz and Begay 1994).
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Other health policy research has indicated that success in the legislative arena requires advocates to have power (the ability to translate ideas into action), ideas (solid plans of what to do and ways of framing issues), and leadership (the ability to recognize opportunities and commitment to challenge status quo) (Oliver and Paul-Shaheen 1997). In terms of Proposition 99, this meant that advocates needed to exploit their chief source of power-public sentiment-in order to leverage the political process. They also needed to have a strong idea of how the money should be spent, reflecting a theory of why the tobacco problem exists and what would alleviate it (Weiss and Tschirhart 1994). They would be fighting for revenues against some very sophisticated opponents who would know where they wanted the money to go. Advocates also needed to frame the political debate over Proposition 99 as "follow the will of the voter," a simple message that demanded respect for the democratic process. The effort to control the "ideas" of the debate was likely to be complicated by the fact that there were multiple organizations competing for the Proposition 99 money, each with its own policy ideas and its own way to frame the problem. These other organizations would also understand that issues are often won and lost based on how they are framed (Riker 1986). Finally, they needed to find effective leadership.

reference: Pierce JP, Gilpin EA, Emery SL, White MM, Rosbrook B, Berry CC. Has the California Tobacco Control Program Reduced Smoking? JAMA : the journal of the American Medical Association. 1998;280(10):893-9.

activity_script_v1_1: A central theme of the media campaign was reversing the erroneous image that smoking is glamorous that was created through decades of tobacco industry advertising. Campaigns that focused on minorities and youth began in May 1990 and continued throughout the summer months. Television, radio, print, and outdoor advertising are being used to target, in successive priority, the following population groups: children aged 11 to 14 years; children aged 6 to 10 years; adolescents and young adults aged 15 to 21 years; adult smokers; pregnant women who smoke; and individuals, primarily men, who experiment with or regularly use smokeless tobacco. Assembly Bill 75 allowed the California Department of Health Services (CDHS) to use whatever media it determined to be most effective in convincing Californians, especially adolescents, not to smoke, and stipulated that current market research and surveys be used in media development. In addition to electronic media, outdoor advertising in shopping malls and on billboards and buses and exposures through the print media for younger children and specific minority groups are planned. In terms of the dollars being spent, California's campaign is the largest such endeavor ever undertaken by any governmental agency. It is also unique in that (1) the campaign is authorized to use paid advertising to promote media messages; (2) it comprises a full range of communications strategies, including public and community relations; and (3) the creative teams have experience in promoting healthy behaviors through advertising as well as in counteracting explicit and implicit health-risk images portrayed by the media. The CDHS is not using its media funds to supplant tobacco industry support of the arts, sports, or other community events.
barriers_v1_1_v2: Funding for the media campaign was vetoed by the governor in 1992 and later restored, but it was reinstated at a lower level than previously. Also, the administration has been accused of "watering down" the antismoking advertising.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Antismoking television ads focused on the duplicity of the tobacco industry and the dangers of secondhand tobacco smoke.

activity_script_v1_1: During the first and second year, students hung up self-made antismoking posters in public places. Students were also given an opportunity to participate in no-smoking competitions. Parish confirmation camps were included into the programme during the third year. Camp leaders were informed about the programme and trained to motivate students to do anti-smoking activities.

reference: Vartiainen E, Pennanen M, Haukkala A, Dijk F, Lehtovuori R, De Vries H. The effects of a three-year smoking prevention programme in secondary schools in Helsinki. Eur J Public Health. 2007 Jun;17(3):249-56. doi: 10.1093/eurpub/ckl107. Epub 2007 Jan 5. PMID: 17208954.
activity_script_v1_1:
The programme included 14 information lessons about smoking, and refusal skills training led by an outside drama group. Smoking prevention was also integrated into regular subjects such as maths, Finnish and geography. In the third year, the school nurses taught one smoking cessation lesson.

Five 1-h lessons in 13 experimental schools provided information on: smoking prevention in general; how to say no; the consequences of smoking and reasons for smoking; development of refusal skills; and opinions and reasons for non-smoking. A video demonstrated social influence processes. In the second lesson, a drama group demonstrated social influence resistance skills. The young people practiced refusal skills in three role-plays. They did not receive a student manual. In one lesson, pupils gave reasons for their decision to be non-smokers. In the last lesson, the presented their reasons for non-smoking. In the spring of 1999, smoking was discussed in four other lessons, such as mathematics and geography.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: The strength of this programme was the collaboration of various bodies. The Helsinki educational administration and the principals in different schools supported the programme and made it possible to successfully complete the study.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2: Regarding the drama group teaching refusal skills: students provided such positive feedback that it is recommended for schools to include such activities in their budget planning.

reference: Vartiainen E, Pennanen M, Haukkala A, Dijk F, Lehtovuori R, De Vries H. The effects of a three-year smoking prevention programme in secondary schools in Helsinki. Eur J Public Health. 2007 Jun;17(3):249-56. doi: 10.1093/eurpub/ckl107. Epub 2007 Jan 5. PMID: 17208954.
activity_script_v1_1: In the third year, school dentists were involved in the programme as well. During the regular 2-min dental appointments, trained dentists informed students about the hazards of smoking and how smoking affects their gums and teeth. In the third year, the school nurses taught one smoking cessation lesson.

Various local walking groups were organized to encourage an increase in physical activity. Heart-healthy foods and recipes were made available. Fitness activities were held indoors or outdoors, depending on the weather.
'KidsWalk' program was a month-long school walking campaign that encouraged all students, regardless of athletic ability, to take part in regular physical activity. Teachers led student walking groups and recorded the miles covered. The OSHHP compiled weekly mileage reports in an attempt to achieve a month's-end goal.

Each issue of Tips focused on one heart disease risk factor, was 'camera ready' in three sizes for easy duplication, and formatted for inclusion in newsletters. Fridge Facts were 8.5 x 11inch paper posters to place on bulletin boards and refrigerators and distributed throughout the community.

activity_script_v1_1: A two county-wide Advisory Council was formed as risk factor-specific task forces (nutrition, physical activity, tobacco awareness). Community leaders involved in program initiatives included representatives from the county health departments, the Cornell Cooperative Extension, local hospitals, and many other voluntary organizations. Twenty-four local health committees were established in villages and townships to plan activities aimed at reducing Cardiovascular risk factors.
