activity_script_v1_1: In the Healthy Heart Hometown concept, outreach activities were directed sequentially at every town and village. Local health concerns specific to each township were addressed and a menu of cardiovascular interventions was offered (slide presentations, restaurant/grocery store programs) for which the project staff could provide technical assistance.

reference: Nafziger AN, Erb TA, Jenkins PL, Lewis C, Pearson TA. The Otsego-Schoharie healthy heart program: prevention of cardiovascular disease in the rural US. Scand J Public Health Suppl. 2001;56:21-32.
activity_script_v1_1: One full-time and one part-time community organizer were hired to survey and organize the local communities. In addition, a full-time health educator and exercise physiologist were hired to coordinate workplace screenings and walking groups. A full-time media person was hired to create media spots and develop educational materials. By the end of the first year, a part-time dietitian was working on the nutritional programs. Throughout the program, there was heavy utilization of lay volunteers, directed by the small interdisciplinary staff of six.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: Community organization in rural areas requires interaction with a large number of organizations and must begin at village rather than county level. Local leadership, local participation, and community involvement in choice of interventions are essential.

reference: Nafziger AN, Erb TA, Jenkins PL, Lewis C, Pearson TA. The Otsego-Schoharie healthy heart program: prevention of cardiovascular disease in the rural US. Scand J Public Health Suppl. 2001;56:21-32.
activity_script_v1_1:
Material was adapted from the American Heart Association School Site Kits for its school presentations, modifying them for each grade level. Program staff members, including a health educator, a registered dietitian, and a media specialist, adapted material that included 'Smoke Is No Joke,' 'Journey Into The Heart', 'Face The Fats', and 'Snack Attack.' Whenever possible, the presentations were scheduled to coincide with community-sponsored cardiovascular risk factor screenings, a key component of the community organization process; children took home flyers promoting these events.

An emphasis was placed on kindergarten through eighth grade.

Project S.T.A.R.S. was a tobacco education program that discouraged young people from using tobacco.

reference: Nafziger AN, Erb TA, Jenkins PL, Lewis C, Pearson TA. The Otsego-Schoharie healthy heart program: prevention of cardiovascular disease in the rural US. Scand J Public Health Suppl. 2001;56:21-32.
activity_script_v1_1: The Friends for Life Assembly was an introductory presentation on cardiovascular risk factors and included a discussion about the heart and behaviors that students could change to lower their risk of heart disease. It included a trio of costumed, cartoon characters (Low Fat Cat, Jogger T. Gerbil, and Butt-Out Bunny) designed by a local artist and made by a local theatrical designer. Presentations were done with the help of volunteers recruited from among the parents, faculty, and students of each school.

reference: Nafziger AN, Erb TA, Jenkins PL, Lewis C, Pearson TA. The Otsego-Schoharie healthy heart program: prevention of cardiovascular disease in the rural US. Scand J Public Health Suppl. 2001;56:21-32.
activity_script_v1_1: Project S.T.A.R.S. was a tobacco education program that encouraged merchants to protect young people by not selling tobacco products to persons under the age of 18 (which is the law).

reference: Nafziger AN, Erb TA, Jenkins PL, Lewis C, Pearson TA. The Otsego-Schoharie healthy heart program: prevention of cardiovascular disease in the rural US. Scand J Public Health Suppl. 2001;56:21-32.
activity_script_v1_1: As there are no local television stations, this medium was not considered cost-effective. Instead, mass media strategies focused on radio and newspapers. In addition, special forms of media exist in rural communities and were effective for local communications; the program developed an effective and low-cost educational tool by enlisting the help of community groups surveyed during the initial community assessment. Media efforts, including radio spots, newspaper articles, and brochures, helped to increase awareness of the program and to promote cardiovascular health. These efforts were specially geared toward the rural population.

reference: Nafziger AN, Erb TA, Jenkins PL, Lewis C, Pearson TA. The Otsego-Schoharie healthy heart program: prevention of cardiovascular disease in the rural US. Scand J Public Health Suppl. 2001;56:21-32.
activity_script_v1_1: Neighborhood and workplace screenings were conducted to assess cardiovascular risk factors for all workplaces with at least 25 workers. Program staff assessed cardiovascular risk factors and offered personalized counseling concerning these risk factors (serum cholesterol, blood pressure levels, smoking, body weight, and physical activity). Each participant was given a risk factor results form with information about each risk factor and a risk factor goal (e.g. blood pressure <140/90mmHg), as well as follow-up recommendations.

reference: Pentz MA, MacKinnon DP, Dwyer JH, Wang EY, Hansen WB, Flay BR, et al. Longitudinal effects of the midwestern prevention project on regular and experimental smoking in adolescents. Prev Med. 1989;18(2):304-21.
activity: 2.1 Establishing a coalition and building its capacity
activity_script_v1_1: The needs assessment followed by introductory training and establishment of a community coordinating structure with school and community leaders to plan program implementation.

reference: Pentz MA, MacKinnon DP, Dwyer JH, Wang EY, Hansen WB, Flay BR, et al. Longitudinal effects of the midwestern prevention project on regular and experimental smoking in adolescents. Prev Med. 1989;18(2):304-21.
activity_script_v1_1:
Project STAR (Students Taught Awareness and Resistance). STAR consisted of introduction of each program component: a 10-session school program for resisting and counteracting drug use influences, and a 5-session booster program for maintaining resistance skills practice and counteracting influences anticipated in the transition to high school.

The school and booster programs were 45-50 minutes in duration each, delivered in regular health, science, or social studies classes by teachers, using modeling, role-playing, group discussion, and peer facilitation methods of instruction.

reference: Pentz MA, MacKinnon DP, Dwyer JH, Wang EY, Hansen WB, Flay BR, et al. Longitudinal effects of the midwestern prevention project on regular and experimental smoking in adolescents. Prev Med. 1989;18(2):304-21.
activity_script_v1_1:
A minimum of three planning meetings and one educational seminar per year, organizing to change school policies
about institutionalizing drug prevention curricula and restricting drug use in and around schools.

reference: Pentz MA, MacKinnon DP, Dwyer JH, Wang EY, Hansen WB, Flay BR, et al. Longitudinal effects of the midwestern prevention project on regular and experimental smoking in adolescents. Prev Med. 1989;18(2):304-21.
activity_script_v1_1:
Teacher training and student leader training in drug prevention skills and program delivery. Teachers were trained by project health educator staff in an initial three-day workshop and a one day refresher workshop. The workshops included an overview of theory, scheduling, and demonstration and practice of sessions and delivery methods.

Training for the parent program consisted of a one-day workshop each year for principals, parent group representatives, and student leaders. Training focused on changing school policy regarding prevention education, initiating smoking and alcohol controls in and around schools, and implementing seminars for all parents on
parent-child communication and prevention support skills.

reference: Pentz MA, MacKinnon DP, Dwyer JH, Wang EY, Hansen WB, Flay BR, et al. Longitudinal effects of the midwestern prevention project on regular and experimental smoking in adolescents. Prev Med. 1989;18(2):304-21.
activity_script_v1_1: The parent program consisted of 3-6 organizational meetings per year, support activities for the school, and an educational seminar for all parents. The companion homework sessions interviewing involved parents and family members about rules on drug use, successful techniques for avoiding drug use, and methods for family counteraction of media and community influences to use drugs. Homework was assigned as part of classroom program sessions, with material collected and discussed in subsequent classroom sessions.

reference: Pentz MA, MacKinnon DP, Dwyer JH, Wang EY, Hansen WB, Flay BR, et al. Longitudinal effects of the midwestern prevention project on regular and experimental smoking in adolescents. Prev Med. 1989;18(2):304-21.
activity_script_v1_1:
Mass media coverage of the program consisted of newspaper articles and 1- to 2-minute television news clips of project staff announcing the purpose and direction of the project, coverage of teacher training and classroom sessions, television and radio talk show interviews with project staff about program progress, and a televised press conference reporting baseline rates of drug use in the Kansas City area. The news clips were aired on the 5 p.m. and 6 p.m. news by the three major network stations, television and radio talk shows with project staff, and a
televised press conference.

reference: Wen X, Chen W, Gans KM, Colby SM, Lu C, Liang C, et al. Two-year effects of a school-based prevention programme on adolescent cigarette smoking in Guangzhou, China: a cluster randomized trial. Int J Epidemiol. 2010;39(3):860-76.
activity_script_v1_1: We edited and distributed a 20-page anti-smoking textbook including information on the history of tobacco, harmful ingredients in tobacco, consequences of adolescent smoking, coping strategies for anxiety and depression, resistance skills for handling peer pressure to smoke, tips for smoking cessation and information on 'World No Tobacco Day'
satistfaction_v1_1_v2: Students showed low interest in the "signature on the 18th World Day of No Smoking" (33.0%). The extent of students' general satisfaction to the program was 52.4%.

reference: Wen X, Chen W, Gans KM, Colby SM, Lu C, Liang C, et al. Two-year effects of a school-based prevention programme on adolescent cigarette smoking in Guangzhou, China: a cluster randomized trial. Int J Epidemiol. 2010;39(3):860-76.
activity_script_v1_1: We delivered four-page anti-smoking pamphlets to students, their parents and teachers.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2: The students did not show great interest in  "health pamphlets" (26.6%) or "specific textbooks" (25.9%)

reference: Wen X, Chen W, Gans KM, Colby SM, Lu C, Liang C, et al. Two-year effects of a school-based prevention programme on adolescent cigarette smoking in Guangzhou, China: a cluster randomized trial. Int J Epidemiol. 2010;39(3):860-76.
activity_script_v1_1:
Based on a four-page anti-smoking pamphlet and and other smoking-related information students found on the Internet, students created an information area about smoking and health on the blackboard in each classroom.

School nurses and health education teachers attended an 8-h training workshop conducted by project investigators and health education experts and then implemented the anti-smoking health curriculum, which included: (i) a lecture on short-term and long-term consequences of smoking; (ii) a film that included interviews with patients who had smoking-associated illnesses; (iii) an animal experiment demonstrating the biological effects of the harmful ingredients in tobacco;33 (iv) a panel discussion on the health impacts of smoking and anti-smoking experience among a health educator (instructor), an adult ex-smoker and two students (a current smoker and a non-smoker); (v) use of role play scenarios to demonstrate and practice resistance skills for handling peer pressure; and (vi) discussion of coping strategies for anxiety and depression.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
Current private schools may provide inadequate smoking-related knowledge to their students. Adding to or intensifying the existing anti-smoking health curriculum in private schools can effectively improve their students' smoking-related knowledge.

Individual differences in health knowledge, attitudes and skills might not have been adequately addressed using
a school-based rather than an individually tailored intervention.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2: The top seven activities in which students showed greatest interests were "the nicotine toxicity experiment" (64.5%), "signature on the 18th World Day of No Smoking" (33.0%), "health education through experiments" (31.2%), "health education through multimedia" (29.8%), "class meetings with a thesis of smoking" (26.8%), "health pamphlets" (26.6%), "specific textbooks" (25.9%). The extent of students' general satisfaction to the work of tobacco control in school during the last year was 52.4%.

reference: Wen X, Chen W, Gans KM, Colby SM, Lu C, Liang C, et al. Two-year effects of a school-based prevention programme on adolescent cigarette smoking in Guangzhou, China: a cluster randomized trial. Int J Epidemiol. 2010;39(3):860-76.
activity_script_v1_1:
A large educational poster promoting the enforcement of the World Health Organization's Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (27 February 2005) was posted in the schools. We also held a school-wide ceremony for the '18th World No Tobacco Day' (31 May 2005) and exhibited photographs of tobacco-related diseases and actual pathological organs to realistically demonstrate the negative health effects of smoking. After the ceremony, Students were encouraged to sign a public commitment not to smoke.

In each intervention school classroom, a contest was held for selecting the best anti-smoking essays and presentations. The best three from each class got rewards and the winner became the candidate for a school-wide contest.

A contest was held in which students competed to make the best anti-smoking posters at both the classroom and
school levels. The best products were framed and hung on the walls of the school buildings.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
Current private schools may provide inadequate smoking-related knowledge to their students. Adding to or intensifying the existing anti-smoking health curriculum in private schools can effectively improve their students' smoking-related knowledge.

Individual differences in health knowledge, attitudes and skills might not have been adequately addressed using
a school-based rather than an individually tailored intervention.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2: The top seven activities in which students showed greatest interests were "the nicotine toxicity experiment" (64.5%), "signature on the 18th World Day of No Smoking" (33.0%), "health education through experiments" (31.2%), "health education through multimedia" (29.8%), "class meetings with a thesis of smoking" (26.8%), "health pamphlets" (26.6%), "specific textbooks" (25.9%). The extent of students' general satisfaction to the work of tobacco control in school during the last year was 52.4%.

reference: Wen X, Chen W, Gans KM, Colby SM, Lu C, Liang C, et al. Two-year effects of a school-based prevention programme on adolescent cigarette smoking in Guangzhou, China: a cluster randomized trial. Int J Epidemiol. 2010;39(3):860-76.
activity_script_v1_1:
A smoking prevention committee was established, which consisted of the school principal, school nurses, the chief director of each grade and a research assistant. The smoking prevention committee reviewed and revised existing school smoking prevention policies. Two key components in the new policies were: (i) a school-wide smoking ban, e.g. 'nobody is allowed to smoke anywhere on campus', and (ii) an explicit commitment on the part of the school to support an anti-smoking initiative, e.g. 'the school should guarantee nonacademic time, faculty and a budget for anti-smoking health education and student activities'.

'No-smoking' signs were posted on campus to reinforce the anti-smoking school policies. In addition, two versions of antismoking advertisements were posted on the walls of school buildings, including one showing a famous
Chinese gymnast crushing cigarettes. 

A large educational poster promoting the enforcement of the World Health Organization's Framework Convention on Tobacco Control (27 February 2005) was posted in the schools.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2: The extent of students' general satisfaction to the work of tobacco control in school during the last year was 52.4%.

reference: Wen X, Chen W, Gans KM, Colby SM, Lu C, Liang C, et al. Two-year effects of a school-based prevention programme on adolescent cigarette smoking in Guangzhou, China: a cluster randomized trial. Int J Epidemiol. 2010;39(3):860-76.
activity_script_v1_1: We sent a formal letter to students' parents, pointing out the adverse consequences of smoking on adolescents, parental influences on adolescent smoking and the importance of parental participation. Attached to the letter was a pre-printed contract to create a smoke-free family. Briefly, this contract required parents to not smoke at home, try to quit smoking, reduce access to cigarettes at home, limit children's spending money, communicate with children about not smoking, supervise their smoking behaviours and assist students in refusing cigarettes. The contract also listed the student's responsibilities (e.g. say 'no' to offered cigarettes, persuade and help smoking parents to quit). The signed contract was posted at home.
risks_v1_1_v2: This activity was not among the top seven activities in which students showed greatest interest.

reference: Wen X, Chen W, Gans KM, Colby SM, Lu C, Liang C, et al. Two-year effects of a school-based prevention programme on adolescent cigarette smoking in Guangzhou, China: a cluster randomized trial. Int J Epidemiol. 2010;39(3):860-76.
activity_script_v1_1:
To reduce cigarette availability, we attempted to reduce sales of cigarettes to minors in the area surrounding the
intervention schools. With the help of community leaders, we educated the owners of four grocery stores near the activity school not to sell cigarettes to students (e.g. the sale of single or unpacked cigarettes) and secured commitments from them to abide by this policy.
barriers_v1_1_v2: We were unable to implement this intervention component in stores around one private school due to the hesitation of school administrators.

activity_script_v1_1:
Community
group involvement consisted of project staff visits to all
Parents and Citizens groups of the intervention schools
(n=13) and a Rotaract group. Strategies for reducing
the supply of cigarettes to minors were discussed, with
all participants invited to a community forum which
was also attended by local police; Public Health Unit
and project staff. The forum was designed to generate
a local plan for reducing young people's access to
tobacco through retailer education.

 The local Public Health Unit was also asked to
provide surveillance of those retailers through public
complaints about suspected illegal sales to minors and
initiate prosecutions of retailers where appropriate.

The Why Smoke? smoking prevention package" Jointly
developed by the New South Wales Quit Campaign and
the Department of School Education) was used as the
standard school-based intervention component.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
Maintaining teacher enthusiasm in the intervention
group schools. Despite strong support, some teachers
found it hard to thoroughly implement the Why
Smoke? program given competing demands.

Most teachers reported that they had
implemented the program, but in spite of this, minimal
changes are evident. It was possible that the schoolbased
component could have been better implemented
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
The Why smoke package was chosen because of its wide acceptance by
schools.

It was only due to the extremely positive attitudes on
the part of school staff that project staff were able to
enlist the support of teachers to be involved in the
teaching of sequential smoking prevention sessions
with reference to the guidelines given.

activity_script_v1_1:
The parental support component: This component of
the project aimed to develop and strengthen parents'
skills to assist their children to remain non-smokers.
All parents of students in intervention schools were
given an information kit which included information
relating to physiological effects of smoking and passive
smoking, and examples of how parental attitudes and
behaviours support children to be non-smokers.
Parents were also supplied with a contact phone
number to obtain a Quit kit. This information was
disseminated via fliers, leaflets and letters. A summarv
was distributed to parents about six weeks after th~
distribution of the kit. Also, three parent information
evenings were organised at three school venues (of a
possible 13 schools). Sites were selected for ease of
access by public and private transport. The evenings
were extensively promoted, including that a drug and
alcohol counsellor would be present and that parents
could meet other parents to discuss their concerns
about young people and smoking.
barriers_v1_1_v2:
Surprisingly, project
staff reported that parents either did not feel that their
children were likely to become smokers or that the
degree of danger was not a high priority for them.


It was a common view among parents that if all their
children did was to smoke a cigarette, then they really
did not have much to worry about.

PILOT TESTING: The lack of rigorous field testing of the various
interventions was another contributing factor leading
to the project's disappointing results. This is especially
true with regard to the parental component. Health
promotion units at Area Health Service level should
use their core staff funding for this kind of piloting and
field testing with follow-up impact studies in order to be
able to put together a substantial campaign.

barriers_v1_1_v2:

activity: 2.1 Establishing a coalition and building its capacity
activity_script_v1_1:
Sister paper: 

Local coalitions for cardiovascular disease (CVD) prevention were formed across the six Bootheel counties in response to mortality data, limited access to medical services, and identified community needs. A regional project coordinator facilitated community engagement by involving local organizations and leaders in planning and securing broad stakeholder support. Coalition members contributed their perspectives on community CVD needs, and the coalitions used available data to establish objectives and intervention strategies, building on existing programs where feasible.
Coalition development was supported through the involvement of local leaders and community organizations in the planning process. Leaders were identified via established agencies and community referrals, with the project coordinator maintaining a detailed contact inventory.
facilitators_v1_1_v2: The observed reductions in physical inactivity and increases in cholesterol screening in the Bootheel project indicate that community‑level cardiovascular risk can be reduced through relatively low‑cost interventions that integrate education with environmental modifications.
risks_v1_1_v2: All risk factors, except cigarette smoking, showed slightly more favorable prevalence rates among respondents who were aware of the heart health coalition.
participation_v1_1_v2: Community interventions may produce benefits unrelated to cardiovascular disease risk. In the Bootheel project, coalition members became more engaged in civic life, including being elected to city councils and school boards.

The same school‑based program was delivered in all four communities. A common curriculum for students in grades five to ten was created using research with the target group and proven smoking‑prevention programs. The program included age‑appropriate lesson plans, teaching materials, teacher training, and checks to ensure it was carried out consistently.
This curriculum was delivered alongside the existing school programs that focused on preventing alcohol and other drug use.
barriers_v1_1_v2: Students who received both the school and media program were more likely to use smokeless tobacco. This was unexpected, because it did not happen during or right after the program. One possible reason is that the program focused strongly on stopping cigarette smoking, which may have made smokeless tobacco seem less harmful by comparison. The researchers note that this needs more study.

reference: Storr, C. L., Ialongo, N. S., Kellam, S. G., & Anthony, J. C. (2002). A randomized controlled trial of two primary intervention strategies to prevent early onset tobacco smoking. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 66(1), 51-60. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0376-8716(01)00184-3
activity_script_v1_1:
The classroom‑centered (CC) intervention was made up of three integrated elements: curricular enrichment, strengthened classroom behavior management, and supplementary approaches for students who did not respond adequately to the core strategies. Each classroom was organized into three mixed‑ability groups, which formed the foundation for both instructional and behavior‑management activities. Standard language arts and mathematics curricula were augmented with additional materials designed to foster higher‑order thinking, writing proficiency, and listening and comprehension skills.
Classroom management practices were reinforced through the implementation of the Good Behavior Game (GBG), a whole‑class behavioral strategy shown to be effective in prior research. 
For children who showed limited responsiveness to the GBG, additional support measures were introduced. These included placing such students on individual teams, thereby creating a more direct connection between their behavior and corresponding consequences. This individualized approach was intended to strengthen learning of appropriate classroom behaviors through clearer reinforcement contingencies.

reference: Storr, C. L., Ialongo, N. S., Kellam, S. G., & Anthony, J. C. (2002). A randomized controlled trial of two primary intervention strategies to prevent early onset tobacco smoking. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 66(1), 51-60. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0376-8716(01)00184-3

The family-school partnership (FSP) intervention sought to improve academic and behavioral outcomes by strengthening parent-teacher communication and supporting parents with effective behavior‑management strategies. A central component of the intervention was training teachers and school staff to enhance parent-teacher communication and foster collaborative partnerships.
The Parents on Your Side program 
formed the basis for training teachers in partnership
building and parent-teacher communication.

reference: Storr, C. L., Ialongo, N. S., Kellam, S. G., & Anthony, J. C. (2002). A randomized controlled trial of two primary intervention strategies to prevent early onset tobacco smoking. Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 66(1), 51-60. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0376-8716(01)00184-3

The intervention comprised staff training in parent-teacher communication, ongoing home-school activities, and parent workshops led by school staff.
The parent workshop component began after baseline assessments in Grade 1 and consisted of seven weekly sessions, followed by two booster workshops later in the school year. Initial sessions focused on building strong parent-school partnerships, while subsequent workshops addressed effective discipline and behavior management using a video‑based discussion program. Workshops were led by school psychologists or social workers, and a dedicated phone line was established to support ongoing parent-school communication

activity: 2.1 Establishing a coalition and building its capacity
activity_script_v1_1:
Massachusetts Department of Public Health (MDPH) Massachusetts Division of the American Cancer
Society (ACS), The campaign's success has been credited to several factors: an effective coalition led, heavily
staffed, and funded by the ACS, which attracted over 250 other organizations to the initiative.
ACS logo with its high name recognition and public trust; visibility of volunteer health
professionals; expert legal assistance; grassroots support; and media backing (such as supportive editorials from the state's
leading newspapers)

 
MTCP operationalized these efforts
through a system of six regional networks that linked community-
based programs for the state's six million people.
1-funding and supporting
local boards of health and health departments.

Statewide support of local tobacco control was also manifest in other ways. The geographically-based Tobacco Free
Community Mobilization Networks supported grassroots
coalitions that raised local awareness and catalyzed local
policy changes. The Community Assistance Statewide Team
(CAST) offered legal assistance to localities creating and
enforcing smoke-free ordinances and regulations, with an
eye toward preventing challenges from the tobacco industry.

reference: Weintraub JM & Hamilton WL 2002, 'Trends in prevalence of current smoking, Massachusetts and states without tobacco control programmes, 1990 to 1999', Tobacco Control, 11(2), pp.8-13.
activity_script_v1_1:
A major thrust of MTCP was funding and supporting local boards of health and health departments. Such boards
and departments, typically underfunded and understaffed, welcomed the resources and opportunity to promote tobacco control at the local level. Specifically, they enacted and enforced local ordinances and regulations that prevented youth access to tobacco products and protected the public from ETS. As a result, Massachusetts cities and towns accelerated passage of ordinances and regulations that restricted tobacco sales to young people and increased levels of compliance to these regulations by tobacco merchants.

reference: Weintraub JM & Hamilton WL 2002, 'Trends in prevalence of current smoking, Massachusetts and states without tobacco control programmes, 1990 to 1999', Tobacco Control, 11(2), pp.8-13.
activity_script_v1_1:
Two recent battles on the policy front include the legal challenge by Massachusetts and other states
against the tobacco industry for misrepresenting "light" and "Low tar" cigarettes to the public as safer
and charges by public health leaders and tobacco control advocates that new candy-flavored cigarettes are targeted towards kids and violate the 1998 Master Settlement Agreement (MSA).

Restrictions to prevent tobacco use in young people: Operation Storefront (driven by Youth Young Action Alliances) a 1998 survey of externally visible storefront tobacco advertisements that later prompted action by the state's attorney general to regulate advertisements within 1,000 feet of schools or playgrounds.

reference: Weintraub JM & Hamilton WL 2002, 'Trends in prevalence of current smoking, Massachusetts and states without tobacco control programmes, 1990 to 1999', Tobacco Control, 11(2), pp.8-13.
activity_script_v1_1:
Increased state cigarette excise tax, which began in 1992, which generated new revenue, funded tobacco control programs and also discouraged consumption. 
1992: Increases in the state cigarette excise tax, beginning in 1992, generated new revenue, funded tobacco control programs,
and also discouraged consumption. (Ballot initiative raises cigarette excise tax by $.25, creating revenue to fund Massachusetts Tobacco Control Program (MTCP).

1996: First cigar tax in MA is imposed (15% of wholesale price). In 1996, the state legislature
employed an innovative strategy that increased the tax another $.25 (to $.76 per pack) to fund health care for uninsured children and senior pharmacy services.

After 2002: the cigarette excise tax in Massachusetts was raised again (2002) to $1.51 per pack to increase general revenues.

Smokeless tobacco taxes also rose. After passing legislationto tax smokeless tobacco at 25% of wholesale price in
1986, the state pushed through three more increases (in 1992, 1996, and 2002) through legislative action that drove
the smokeless tobacco tax up to its current level of 90% of wholesale price.

In addition, a 15% tax on the wholesale price of cigars and pipe tobacco initially levied in 1996 later rose to its current level of 30%.

reference: Weintraub JM & Hamilton WL 2002, 'Trends in prevalence of current smoking, Massachusetts and states without tobacco control programmes, 1990 to 1999', Tobacco Control, 11(2), pp.8-13.

1987 Clean indoor air law requires restaurants with 75 or more seats to set aside 200 sq. feet as non-smoking section.
1998 Smoking is prohibited in State House, state buildings, and state vehicles.

2004).

(1995).

activity_script_v1_1:
Media campaign which consisted mainly of television advertising (80%), and other media activities including through radio, billboards, and newspapers.  

It also provided a public counterpoint to the tobacco industry's previously unchallenged promotional advertising, which had
led to smoking initiation among receptive youth.

The media campaign complemented and promoted other MTCP activities. The media efforts sought
to link smokers to treatment services, both through dedicated messages and also by displaying the Quitline number and cessation website address.

In addition, media messages concerning the dangers of secondhand smoke placed the blame on the tobacco industry, not
on smokers, and helped create the atmosphere whereby the issue of clean indoor air was kept alive in the public's consciousness. This helped facilitate the passage of local tobacco control laws and regulations.

Its messages promoted all three major goals for young people, adult smokers, and the general public. The tagline, "It's time we made smoking history," achieved a high level of recognition among the public.
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
Ultimately, constant research and revamping concluded that the most successful approach with both the young and
adults involved "real people telling real stories." MTCP media campaigns tapped into the authenticity of true stories that
were graphic, negative, and emotional to capture the public's attention. The "Truth" campaign featured former employees of the tobacco industry (Wayne McClaren [the late "Marlboro Man"], Janet Sackman [a former cigarette model],
and the late Victor Crawford [an ex-tobacco industry lobbyist]) who as "insiders" described the industry's manipulation
of nicotine in tobacco and their deception of the public. These advertisements were among those rated most effective by both young people and adults interviewed in follow-up surveys. 

Two particularly successful series of advertisements featured state residents who suffered deep personal loss and
tragedy from tobacco addiction. Advertisements featuring Pam Laffin, who received a lung transplant due to emphysema at age 24, and Rick Stoddard, who lost his wife from lung cancer at the age of 46, generated tremendous interest.
Based on the documented success of the "Pam" advertisements among young people,27 the MTCP developed a school
curriculum kit featuring a video documentary about Laffin's saga that reached approximately 150,000 Massachusetts students in grades 6-10.31 Laffin's death in 2000 marked a tragic milestone in this history. Subsequently, this curriculum gained national exposure, with MTV airing the documentary in 2001,32 and the CDC disseminating the teaching module in 2002 to schools throughout the country.
participation_v1_1_v2: Accounted for approximately one-third of the overall program resources (and averaged an annual budget of approximately $13 million from 1994 - 2001.
satistfaction_v1_1_v2:
Longitudinal study data of the reach and penetration of the television advertisements during the first three years (1993
1996) found that 88% of adults and 94% of adolescents reported seeing at least one anti-smoking advertisement on
television during the prior several years. Over half (56%) of the adults and nearly three-fourths (73%) of the young
people reported seeing them on a weekly basis.

Data from the programs' ongoing random digit dial telephone survey also documented broad awareness of the television campaign among target audiences; from 1995 to 2002 (~80-90% ).

Longitudinal study data of the reach and penetration of the television advertisements during the first three years (1993
1996) found that 88% of adults and 94% of adolescents reported seeing at least one anti-smoking advertisement on
television during the prior several years. Over half (56%) of the adults and nearly three-fourths (73%) of the young
people reported seeing them on a weekly basis.

Data from the programs' ongoing random digit dial telephone survey also documented broad awareness of the television campaign among target audiences; from 1995 to 2002 (~80-90% ).

reference: Weintraub JM & Hamilton WL 2002, 'Trends in prevalence of current smoking, Massachusetts and states without tobacco control programmes, 1990 to 1999', Tobacco Control, 11(2), pp.8-13.
activity_script_v1_1:
To provide for more formalized, standards-based training for tobacco
treatment specialists, MTCP contracted with the University
of Massachusetts Medical School in 1997 to develop a
statewide training and certification program,54 the first of its

reference: Weintraub JM & Hamilton WL 2002, 'Trends in prevalence of current smoking, Massachusetts and states without tobacco control programmes, 1990 to 1999', Tobacco Control, 11(2), pp.8-13.
activity_script_v1_1:
Quitline - free trained quit coaches which can be reached online or via phone. 
The Program provided a wide array of cessation services tailored to individuals' needs and readiness to seek treatment. The MTCP model for treatment services changed over time. Initial minimal interventions, such as health fairs offering educational outreach materials, evolved into intensive interventions in medical settings involving trained tobacco treatment specialists.

reference: Weintraub JM & Hamilton WL 2002, 'Trends in prevalence of current smoking, Massachusetts and states without tobacco control programmes, 1990 to 1999', Tobacco Control, 11(2), pp.8-13.
activity_script_v1_1:
Four statewide services exist under the umbrella of
the TryToStop Tobacco Resource Center of Massachusetts:
(1) a toll-free telephone hotline: the Massachusetts Quitline
(1-800-TRY-TO-STOP), (2) an interactive website (www.trytostop.org), (3) a clearinghouse of educational materials,
and (4) a QuitWorks program to connect managed care
providers and their patients with tobacco treatment services.
 
 
The QuitWorks program represents a major public-private partnership. Launched in 2002, it links Massachusetts
physicians in eight major commercial and Medicaid health
plans and their patients who smoke to the statewide cessation
services described above. Its objectives are promoting
provider behavior change, facilitating referrals to treatment,
and providing evidence-based, proactive telephone counseling
services. Participating providers fax an enrollment form
to the Quitline for those patients who agree to make an
attempt to quit at the time of an office visit. In turn, Quitline
staff proactively call these patients to offer cessation services.

barriers_v1_1_v2:
It is possible that non-prosecutory enforcement of public health legislation despite involving local police does not have sufficient impact to reduce smoking prevalence. The intervention could only have been effective if the major
method of students' access to cigarettes was through direct purchase from retailers. While the majority of responses indicated direct retail purchase as the source of students' cigarettes many students also nominated alternative sources such as parents and siblings. Concurrent strategies addressing both the demand for and supply of cigarettes are likely to be required if significant reduction in under-age smoking is to be achieved.

activity_script_v1_1: The grade-specific units focused on smoking- and health-related knowledge, decision‑making skills, strategies for coping with peer, stress, and advertising influences, smoking cessation skills, and strengthening social support for non-smokers. This curriculum was delivered alongside existing programs aimed at preventing alcohol and other drug use.
facilitators_v1_1_v2:
The design of these interventions was based on strategies that often characterize effective health education programs. They were delivered through multiple educational modalities that routinely reach these populations. The interventions began in preadolescence, before substantial proportions of these young people had made decisions about smoking, and they continued for four years during the main period of risk for smoking adoption. The intensity of both the school and the media interventions over this period was high compared with other smoking prevention programs. The interventions also were carefully targeted to the educational needs of well-defined subgroups within the target population. They were based on theory and previous research and additional research with these target populations.

The school interventions were sensitive to changing developmental needs over four years of adolescent growth.

activity_script_v1_1:
The mass media component consisted of television and radio messages ("spots") produced specifically for the study, each lasting 30 or 60 seconds. The spots were designed to address shared program objectives while appealing to the interests of the target audiences. To promote diversity in production style, six producers were commissioned to create 36 television spots and 17 radio spots across multiple formats, including situation comedy, rock video, cartoon, testimonial, and drama. The spots did not share common elements such as slogans, music, actors, or graphics, allowing each message to convey the same educational objectives in a distinct style.

The spots were aired as paid local advertisements on broadcast and cable television and on radio stations. Airtime purchases were supplemented by donated placements from media outlets, increasing overall exposure by approximately 50 percent. As the target audiences matured, the exposure strategy was adjusted by gradually increasing radio placements and reducing television placements.
participation_v1_1_v2: It is possible that emphasis on the undesirability of smoking in the media-school area during the intervention period overshadowed unfavorable messages about smokeless tobacco and diminished their long-term effectiveness.

activity_script_v1_1:
The classroom-centered (CC) intervention was made up of three integrated elements. Within each CC classroom, students were organized into three heterogeneous groups, which formed the foundation for both curricular and behavioral components.

1. Curriculum enhancements: The standard language arts and mathematics curriculum was supplemented with new additional materials and activities. These enhancements were designed to strengthen students' critical thinking, writing, listening, and comprehension skills beyond what was offered in the existing curriculum.

2. Enhanced behavior management practices: Behavior management approaches were expanded through the use of the Good Behavior Game (GBG), a classroom-wide strategy aimed at fostering cooperative problem-solving among students. The GBG seeks to reduce disruptive and aggressive behavior while increasing student engagement and on-task behavior. Students were assigned to one of three heterogeneous teams, and teams earned points when members demonstrated clearly specified positive behaviors. Points were deducted for inappropriate actions, such as being off-task or engaging in conflict. Teams could later exchange points for tangible incentives, including classroom privileges, small items like stickers or erasers, and classroom activities. At the beginning of implementation, game sessions were clearly announced, and rewards were provided immediately afterward. Over time, rewards were delayed until the end of the day or week. Throughout the process, tangible incentives were paired with social reinforcement, with material rewards gradually withdrawn across the school year and replaced by social praise and recognition.

3. Back-up strategies for children not performing adequately: Additional strategies were used for students who did not show sufficient improvement under the GBG structure. These students were placed into individual teams, creating a more direct connection between their behavior and the resulting consequences. This increased clarity was intended to accelerate their learning and adoption of appropriate classroom behavior.

reference: Storr CL, Ialongo NS, Kellam SG, Anthony JC. A randomized controlled trial of two primary school intervention strategies to prevent early onset tobacco smoking. Drug Alcohol Depend. 2002;66(1):51-60.
activity_script_v1_1:
The family-school partnership (FSP) intervention aimed to promote academic success and decrease early manifestations of aggression, withdrawal, and attentional difficulties. These outcomes were pursued through strengthened communication between parents and teachers, as well as by equipping parents with effective instructional techniques and behavior management strategies. A primary strategy for achieving these goals was the provision of specialized training for teachers and relevant school personnel focused on building collaborative parent-teacher relationships. Training in these skills was grounded in the Parents on Your Side program, which served as the core framework for partnership development and communication practices. This training consisted of a three-day seminar, complemented by follow-up supervisory visits, a detailed training manual, and supporting instructional videotapes.

Throughout the intervention period, teachers and school staff met individually with intervention specialists on an as-needed basis to support effective delivery of the program. In addition, teachers participating in the intervention attended monthly group meetings that provided opportunities to address shared challenges and receive continued guidance. To minimize the risk of contamination across study conditions, teachers and school administrators were consistently reminded during planning sessions, training activities, and supervision meetings of the importance of preventing spillover effects that could compromise the study's internal validity. The integrity of both the CC and FSP interventions was supported through the careful specification and standardization of training and intervention protocols. Manuals detailing each intervention were clearly delineated and codified to ensure consistent implementation across contacts. Teachers were also provided with structured materials to facilitate accurate execution of the interventions, including comprehensive outlines and checklists identifying required materials, specified themes or activities, and other relevant implementation guidance. For the CC intervention specifically, implementation fidelity was monitored using three complementary approaches: evaluation of classroom setup, direct classroom observations, and reviews of classroom visit records. Fidelity data were collected during monthly supervisory meetings and during three scheduled classroom visits conducted over the course of the school year.

reference: Storr CL, Ialongo NS, Kellam SG, Anthony JC. A randomized controlled trial of two primary school intervention strategies to prevent early onset tobacco smoking. Drug Alcohol Depend. 2002;66(1):51-60.
activity_script_v1_1:
The family-school partnership (FSP) intervention was developed to enhance academic performance and to reduce early behavioral difficulties, including aggression, social withdrawal, and problems with attention. These objectives were addressed by strengthening communication between parents and teachers and by equipping parents with effective instructional and child behavior management approaches. Two primary mechanisms supported these goals: ongoing weekly home-school learning and communication activities, and a structured series of nine parent workshops facilitated jointly by the first-grade teacher and the school psychologist or social worker. The parent workshop series commenced immediately following pretest assessments in the fall of first grade and was delivered over seven consecutive weeks, with one session held each week through early December. Two additional booster sessions were conducted later in the school year, one during winter and one in spring. The initial two workshops focused on building a strong, sustainable partnership between families and school personnel, laying the groundwork for collaborative efforts to support children's learning and behavior. The subsequent five sessions concentrated on effective discipline and behavior management strategies. This component was guided by the Parents and Children program, a videotape-based modeling and group discussion series. Workshops were led by each school's psychologist or social worker and addressed topics such as the use of praise, parent-child play, setting limits, the use of time-out as an alternative to spanking, and problem-solving skills. In addition to the workshop series, each school implemented a voicemail-based "Warm Line" to encourage continued parent engagement and to support ongoing parent-school communication regarding academic or behavioral concerns.

To support implementation of the FSP intervention, intervention staff were required to document all contacts with parents. Documentation included, where relevant, summaries of the interaction, distinctive features of the contact, duration, parent and child attendance, and levels of participation and completion of assigned home activities. Parents participating in the intervention provided anonymous feedback regarding their use of the strategies taught and the perceived usefulness of those techniques, as well as evaluations of the interpersonal and instructional skills of the family intervention staff. Fidelity to the intervention protocol was further assessed through live and audio-recorded observations of parent workshops, which were used to evaluate adherence to and quality of program delivery.
